_meta.note: Fields: type, path, description, entrypoint (optional), principle, pros, cons. Principle/pros/cons are consumed by the teacher agent when learning_mode=true.

pytorch_template.description: Full PyTorch training project scaffold (trainer, data loader, model, utils, requirements).

label_smoothing.description: Loss | Soft label criterion — smooths hard labels to prevent overconfidence.
label_smoothing.principle: Replaces hard one-hot targets with a convex combination of the true label (weight 1-α) and a uniform distribution over all classes (weight α/C). This reduces the KL divergence penalty on low-probability classes.
label_smoothing.pros: Reduces overfitting on noisy labels; improves calibration (better NLL vs accuracy gap); simple to implement, negligible compute overhead.
label_smoothing.cons: Optimal α is dataset-dependent (typical 0.1); may hurt when labels are already clean and highly informative; slightly slower convergence.

label_smoothing_in_model.description: Loss | Variant that applies label smoothing inside the model's forward pass.
label_smoothing_in_model.principle: Same as label_smoothing but the smoothing is computed within the model (e.g., as a regularization term in the forward), avoiding the need to pre-process targets.
label_smoothing_in_model.pros: Cleaner integration with custom models; avoids changing the dataset.
label_smoothing_in_model.cons: Tightly coupled to model architecture; less flexible than a standalone loss module.

mixup.description: Augmentation | Linear interpolation of input pairs and their labels.
mixup.principle: Generates virtual training examples by mixing two images (x_i, y_i) and (x_j, y_j) with a random λ ∈ [0,1]: x̂ = λx_i + (1-λ)x_j, ŷ = λy_i + (1-λ)y_j. Forces the model to behave linearly between training samples.
mixup.pros: Improves generalization and robustness to adversarial examples; reduces memorization of training samples; works with any architecture.
mixup.cons: Mixed labels are soft (regression-style) which may not align with hard-label evaluation metrics; requires tuning λ sampling strategy; less effective for small datasets.

l1_regularization.description: Regularization | L1 (Lasso) penalty on model weights.
l1_regularization.principle: Adds λ·Σ|w| to the loss, encouraging many weights to become exactly zero — producing sparse models. Useful for feature selection and compressing models.
l1_regularization.pros: Produces sparse solutions (interpretable, low memory); handles correlated features by picking one arbitrarily.
l1_regularization.cons: Non-differentiable at zero (subgradient needed); convergence is slower than L2; sensitive to λ choice.

no_weight_decay_bias.description: Optimizer | Exclude bias and final-layer parameters from weight decay.
no_weight_decay_bias.principle: Standard weight decay is applied uniformly to all parameters, but bias terms have no regularizing prior and final classification layers often need less regularization. This snippet applies decay only to weight matrices.
no_weight_decay_bias.pros: Standard practice in modern fine-tuning (BERT, ViT); often gives 0.5-1% accuracy improvement; trivial to implement.
no_weight_decay_bias.cons: Only matters for models with explicit bias terms; marginal effect on small models.

optimizer_chained_update.description: Optimizer | Manually step multiple parameter groups sequentially.
optimizer_chained_update.principle: Divides parameters into groups (e.g., backbone vs. head) and calls optimizer.step() separately so each group can use different LRs or schedules.
optimizer_chained_update.pros: Enables differential learning rates without multiple optimizers; cleaner than per-group optimizers.
optimizer_chained_update.cons: Requires careful LR scheduling per group; more boilerplate than a single optimizer.

lr_decay.description: Scheduler | Step or cosine learning rate decay.
lr_decay.principle: Reduces LR by a factor every N epochs (step decay) or following a cosine curve from max to min. Prevents the optimizer from overshooting near convergence.
lr_decay.pros: Well-established improvement of 1-3% in final accuracy; easy to implement with PyTorch schedulers.
lr_decay.cons: Requires tuning decay steps/epochs; wrong schedule can stall training.

get_current_lr.description: Scheduler | Inspect current learning rate from optimizer state.
get_current_lr.principle: Reads LR directly from optimizer.param_groups[0]['lr'], useful for logging, warmup decisions, or adaptive scheduling.
get_current_lr.pros: No magic; directly inspects the scheduler state.
get_current_lr.cons: Specific to the first param group; for multi-group setups needs a loop.

grad_clip.description: Training | Clip gradient norms to a maximum threshold.
grad_clip.principle: After loss.backward(), torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm) scales gradients so their L2 norm does not exceed max_norm. Prevents exploding gradients, especially in RNNs and deep transformers.
grad_clip.pros: Prevents training divergence from gradient spikes; cheap (O(n) pass); standard in NLP training.
grad_clip.cons: Can mask gradient signal in genuinely large-gradient steps; max_norm is another hyperparameter.

checkpoint_save_load.description: IO | Save and resume training state (model + optimizer + epoch).
checkpoint_save_load.principle: Serializes model.state_dict(), optimizer.state_dict(), epoch, and any scheduler state to a .pth file. Enables resumption after interruption and safe rollback points.
checkpoint_save_load.pros: Essential for long-running training; allows averaging checkpoints from multiple runs.
checkpoint_save_load.cons: Large files (full model + optimizer state); loading a mismatched architecture silently fails.

classification_train.description: Training | Standard classification training loop (forward, loss, backward, step).
classification_train.principle: Minimal end-to-end PyTorch training loop for image classification: model.train(), forward pass, cross-entropy loss, zero_grad(), backward(), step().
classification_train.pros: Readable baseline; easy to extend with augmentation or mixed precision.
classification_train.cons: No tricks; just the skeleton. Not production-ready without logging, early stopping, and validation.

classification_eval.description: Evaluation | Accuracy and F1 evaluation on a test/validation set.
classification_eval.principle: model.eval(); torch.no_grad(); iterate data loader; collect predictions and targets; compute accuracy, precision, recall, F1.
classification_eval.pros: Standard evaluation protocol; identifies overfitting gap.
classification_eval.cons: Does not account for class imbalance unless F1 is used as primary metric.

finetune_fc.description: Finetune | Freeze backbone, train classifier head only.
finetune_fc.principle: Sets requires_grad=False for all parameters except the final linear layer. Only the classifier head is updated — ideal when the backbone is a strong frozen feature extractor (e.g., ImageNet pretrained model).
finetune_fc.pros: Extremely fast fine-tuning (only 1-2% of parameters trained); prevents catastrophic forgetting; good for small datasets.
finetune_fc.cons: Limited if the pretrained features don't transfer to the target domain.

finetune_fc_high_lr_conv_low_lr.description: Finetune | Higher LR for FC head, lower LR for backbone conv layers.
finetune_fc_high_lr_conv_low_lr.principle: Divides parameters into two groups: head (higher LR, e.g., 1e-3) and backbone (lower LR, e.g., 1e-5). The head learns task-specific features while the backbone adapts gradually, avoiding large gradient disruptions.
finetune_fc_high_lr_conv_low_lr.pros: Better than pure freezing; allows backbone to adapt without destabilizing pretrained features; standard BERT fine-tuning practice.
finetune_fc_high_lr_conv_low_lr.cons: Two LRs need tuning; requires parameter group setup.

extract_imagenet_layer_feature.description: Feature | Extract features from a specific layer of an ImageNet pretrained model.
extract_imagenet_layer_feature.principle: Hooks into a intermediate layer (e.g., layer4 of a ResNet) to capture feature maps without a forward pass through the classifier. Useful for transfer learning or similarity search.
extract_imagenet_layer_feature.pros: No training needed; reusable features; constant time per image.
extract_imagenet_layer_feature.cons: Layer choice is task-dependent; feature dimensionality can be large.

extract_imagenet_multi_conv_features.description: Feature | Extract features from multiple convolutional layers.
extract_imagenet_multi_conv_features.principle: Attaches hooks to multiple intermediate layers and concatenates or pools their outputs, providing a multi-scale representation.
extract_imagenet_multi_conv_features.pros: Captures both low-level (edges, textures) and high-level (semantic) features; useful for fine-grained recognition.
extract_imagenet_multi_conv_features.cons: Higher memory cost; more complex downstream processing.

train_visualization.description: Vis | Live plots of loss, accuracy, and LR during training.
train_visualization.principle: Appends scalar values to a list (or writes to tensorboard/event files) every N steps. After training, matplotlib/tensorboard renders the curves.
train_visualization.pros: Catches divergence early; helps compare hyperparameters; free with PyTorch TensorBoard logger.
train_visualization.cons: I/O overhead if logging every step; requires post-processing for final plots.

custom_loss.description: Loss | Custom nn.Module loss wrapper for complex training objectives.
custom_loss.principle: Defines a loss as an nn.Module subclass, enabling it to hold internal state (e.g., running statistics for label smoothing, class frequencies for weighted CE) and be composed with other losses.
custom_loss.pros: Clean integration with training loop; stateful losses can adapt to data; composable (sum of multiple losses).
custom_loss.cons: Requires understanding of PyTorch autograd; internal state needs careful initialization.
